food_name: Har Cheong Gai (Prawn Paste Fried Chicken Wings)

local_names.hindi: झींगा पेस्ट तली हुई चिकन विंग्स
local_names.romanized_local: Haa1 Zoeng3 Gai1 (Cantonese); Xiā Jiàng Jī (Mandarin)

aliases: झींगा पेस्ट तली हुई चिकन विंग्स

hindi: झींगा पेस्ट तली हुई चिकन विंग्स

food_subgroup: Deep-fried marinated chicken wings; double-fried prawn paste (har cheong) chicken

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for har cheong gai. Closest parent concept: deep-fried marinated chicken wings (FOODON:03302051 — fried chicken). No Singapore-specific zi char variant is represented in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken wings, deep-fried — closest FoodEx2 parent used; no exact code for prawn-paste-marinated double-fried variant
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CHW (chicken wings) is the closest defensible parent. No Singapore zi char or prawn-paste-specific FoodEx2 descriptor exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for har cheong gai or prawn paste fried chicken wings.

preparation_method: marinated 24 hours in fermented prawn paste (har cheong), Shaoxing wine, sesame oil, egg, cornstarch
allergens: gluten (cornstarch may contain trace; Shaoxing wine contains wheat)

serving.description: 4 mid-joint chicken wings (zi char restaurant portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard zi char restaurant serving of har cheong gai is approximately 4 mid-joint chicken wings, weighing about 280 g, providing roughly 798 kcal, 50 g protein, 50 g fat, 34 g carbohydrate, and approximately 1,904 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken wing, meat and skin, cooked, fried (SR Legacy #05062) combined with fermented shrimp paste composition and Singapore HPB oil absorption data for deep-fried foods
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Nutrition Information
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation: USDA SR Legacy fried chicken wing (FDC #05062) as the protein/fat/energy base; fermented prawn paste sodium and micronutrient contribution added from USDA FoodData Central fermented shrimp paste entry; HPB Singapore frying oil absorption coefficient applied for total fat. Values represent the composite dish per 100 g edible portion. Confidence: medium-high.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of cornstarch-battered deep-fried foods — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for cornstarch (GI ~85) moderated by high fat and protein content of fried chicken
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for har cheong gai in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at ~55 based on: (1) cornstarch batter coating GI ~85 (University of Sydney GI Database); (2) substantial fat (18 g/100 g) and protein (18 g/100 g) content significantly attenuating postprandial glucose response per published mixed-meal GI literature; (3) fermented prawn paste and Shaoxing wine marinade contributing minimal additional carbohydrate. GL per 280 g serving = (55 × 33.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 18.5. Confidence: 0.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — 680 mg per 100 g; approximately 1,904 mg sodium per standard 280 g serving (95% of WHO 2,000 mg daily limit from a single dish)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should treat har cheong gai as an occasional food only; the 24-hour fermented prawn paste marinade deeply penetrates the meat, making sodium reduction by rinsing or sauce avoidance ineffective — portion should be limited to 1–2 wings maximum per meal and paired with low-sodium foods for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~18.5 per serving) buffered by high fat and protein; primary concern is caloric density (~798 kcal per 280 g serving) contributing to weight gain and insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume har cheong gai occasionally in controlled portions (1–2 wings); the high fat and protein content moderates the glycemic response from the cornstarch batter, but the very high caloric density and sodium load require careful meal planning and blood glucose monitoring post-consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of high sodium (680 mg/100 g), saturated fat (4.5 g/100 g), cholesterol (95 mg/100 g), and trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from double deep-frying in vegetable oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit har cheong gai to rare occasions; the double deep-frying process increases total fat and may introduce oxidised lipids from reused cooking oil at hawker stalls, and the very high per-serving sodium load (≈1,904 mg) directly elevates blood pressure — a primary CVD risk driver.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High omega-6 fatty acid load from deep-frying oil and very high sodium may exacerbate systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should treat har cheong gai as a very occasional food; the high caloric density promotes weight gain increasing joint loading, and reused deep-frying oil at hawker stalls may contain elevated aldehydes and oxidised lipids that contribute to pro-inflammatory pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Har Cheong Gai (虾酱鸡 / 蝦醬雞) is a quintessential Singapore zi char (煮炒) dish of deep-fried chicken wings marinated for 24 hours in fermented prawn paste (har cheong), Shaoxing rice wine, sesame oil, egg, and cornstarch. The wings are double deep-fried: the first fry cooks the meat through, and the second fry blisters and crisps the skin to a deep reddish-brown crust. The fermented prawn paste imparts an intensely savoury, pungent umami depth and a characteristic aromatic fragrance. Texture is crispy-crunchy on the outside with juicy, well-seasoned meat inside. It is traditionally served with a spicy lime chilli dipping sauce. Nutritionally, a standard 4-wing restaurant serving (~280 g) provides approximately 798 kcal, 50 g protein, 50 g fat, 34 g carbohydrate, and a very high ~1,904 mg sodium — representing 95% of the WHO daily sodium limit from a single dish. The high sodium originates from the 24-hour deep-penetrating prawn paste marinade. Saturated fat is 12.6 g per serving. GI is estimated at ~55 (low-medium) due to fat and protein moderating the cornstarch batter's glycemic effect, but glycemic load per serving is ~18.5 (medium). Clinically, this dish is high concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and fat load, and moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to caloric density. Har cheong gai became popular in Singapore's zi char restaurants from the 1980s, rooted in the Cantonese tradition of marinating proteins in fermented seafood pastes. It is also known as prawn paste chicken, shrimp paste fried chicken, hae ko chicken wings, and Ayam Goreng Belacan in Malay.
common_user_queries: how many calories in 4 chicken wings prawn paste

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy fried chicken wing data combined with fermented prawn paste composition and Singapore HPB frying oil absorption data. No single approved-source entry exists for har cheong gai as a composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate. Overall confidence is medium (0.70–0.75).
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the composite har cheong gai dish (chicken wings + prawn paste marinade + absorbed frying oil) is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026; total fat is confirmed at 18.0 g/100 g. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the double-fried composite dish is not reported in the component sources consulted. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in deep-fried chicken wing preparations per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #05062; no plant-derived vitamin C sources are present in the dish. trans_fat_g 0.1 g/100 g reflects minor trans fat from deep-frying vegetable oil at high temperature; this is a confirmed low-level value, not a zero. GI estimated at ~55 using University of Sydney GI Database cornstarch entry as analogous base, moderated by high fat and protein content per mixed-meal GI literature. GL per 280 g serving calculated as (55 × 33.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 18.5. Halal status is false: fermented prawn paste preparations may contain alcohol-based fermentation byproducts and Shaoxing wine (contains alcohol); dish is not certified halal. FoodEx2 code A0CHW used as closest parent — no exact code for prawn-paste double-fried chicken wings exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
